sound_type: sustained guttural roar colony -- dominant bull territorial roar at summer breeding rookery; multiple overlapping bull roars; 100-1,000 Hz, energy concentrated below 500 Hz; 1-5 seconds sustained; loudest pinniped vocalization in PNW; E=8
ecosystem: Rookery -- rocky offshore islets, sea stacks, coastal outcrops used as summer breeding sites; Washington coast and Puget Sound area
season: Summer (breeding season May-July; bulls arrive first, establish territories before females)
conservation_status: ENDANGERED (western stock, Aleutians/Gulf of Alaska). Population declined ~80% from 1970s to 1990s: 140,000-180,000 peak to ~30,000-40,000. ESA 1990 Threatened, 1997 Endangered western stock. Near Threatened (eastern stock; BC to CA, including Washington; recovering). Washington rookeries: Protection Island, Carroll Island, Sea Lion Rock.
milestone: FIRST OTARIIDAE entry at degree 283. Eared seals distinct from Phocidae: external ear pinnae, rear flipper rotation for land locomotion, front flipper swimming. Largest Otariidae species (bulls up to 2,500 lbs, 11 ft). Named for Georg Wilhelm Steller (1709-1746), naturalist on Bering's Second Kamchatka Expedition 1741. Scientific name Eumetopias jubatus: 'broad forehead' (Greek) + 'maned' (Latin).

coordinates.description: Washington coast and Puget Sound area. Rookery sites: Protection Island, Carroll Island, Sea Lion Rock at Point Grenville. Haul-out sites throughout Salish Sea. Eastern stock animals.

acoustic_data.vocalization_type: Sustained guttural territorial roar. Not a bark (short, percussive) -- a held roar of 1-5 seconds from a 1,100-2,500 lb animal.
acoustic_data.frequency_range: 100-1,000 Hz; energy concentrated below 500 Hz. Large body size (massive chest, thick neck, resonant cranial structures) produces fundamentally low-frequency output.
acoustic_data.duration: Individual roars 1-5 seconds or longer. Sustained sequences during territorial confrontation and display.
acoustic_data.colony_sound: Multiple simultaneous bull roars at active rookery; overlapping with female vocalizations (higher-pitched, shorter) and pup contact calls (bleating). Continuous, intense acoustic environment during summer breeding season.
acoustic_data.travel_distance: Low-frequency vocalizations travel farther in both air and water. Bull roars carry considerable distance across open water from rocky rookery sites.
acoustic_data.e8_calibration: E=8: the combination of vocalizer size (largest pinniped in Pacific Northwest), frequency and power, sustained duration, and colony acoustic environment. Not explosive like orca breach (E=9) but continuous and massive. Arc peak for degrees 281-284.

nodes.title: First Otariidae -- Eared Seals Enter Engine
nodes.description: Degree 283 is the first Otariidae entry. Family distinct from Phocidae (true seals): external ear pinnae, rear flipper rotation, front flipper swimming. Pacific Northwest Otariidae: Steller Sea Lion (283) and California Sea Lion (284). Otariidae chapter spans two consecutive degrees.

nodes.title: Bull Roar -- Sustained Low-Frequency Territorial Vocalization
nodes.description: 100-1,000 Hz, energy below 500 Hz. 1-5 seconds sustained. Massive chest and resonant structures of 2,500-lb bull. Colony sound: multiple simultaneous roars. E=8 arc peak. Not a bark -- a roar.

nodes.title: Georg Wilhelm Steller (1709-1746) -- The Naming
nodes.description: German naturalist on Bering's Second Kamchatka Expedition 1741-1742. First European to document North Pacific systematically. Named: Steller Sea Lion, Steller's Jay, Steller's Eider, Steller's Sea Cow (now extinct). Died age 37 in Tyumen, Siberia, 1746. Steller, not Stellar.

nodes.title: Western Stock Endangered -- ~80% Decline
nodes.description: 140,000-180,000 to ~30,000-40,000 from 1970s to 1990s. ESA 1990 Threatened, 1997 Endangered. Causes contested: nutritional stress (junk food hypothesis), killer whale predation, climate-driven prey shifts, fisheries interactions. Eastern stock (including Washington) Near Threatened and recovering.

nodes.title: Rookery -- Territorial Bull System
nodes.description: Bulls arrive May-June, establish territories before females. Harems of 3-20+ females. Bulls fast for weeks. Territorial combat: roaring + visual display + physical combat (neck-biting). Mane protects neck during combat; same anatomy contributes to roar's low-frequency resonance.

cross_references.relationship: Delphinidae E=7 (cetacean, social, acrobatic) → Otariidae E=8 (pinniped, territorial, massive). Family and energy transition. Marine mammal arc ascends.
Second Otariidae entry. E=7. Urban waterfront winter. Bark cacophony vs. bull roar. Energy steps down from E=8 to E=7. Otariidae chapter: two degrees, two species.
E=7 + E=8 = E=15. Confrontational dark feminist punk pop paired with sustained territorial bull roar. Both deliberately, productively uncomfortable. Both sustained. Different registers, same degree.
